codeSnapshot.label: rmsprop.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

model = nn.Linear(10, 1)

# PyTorch RMSProp
optimizer = torch.optim.RMSprop(
    model.parameters(),
    lr=0.001,
    alpha=0.99,     # decay factor (γ) for squared gradient EMA
    eps=1e-8,       # numerical stability
    weight_decay=0, # L2 regularisation
    momentum=0,     # optional momentum term
    centered=False  # if True: normalise by centered variance
)

# Manual implementation
class RMSProp:
    def __init__(self, params, lr=1e-3, alpha=0.99, eps=1e-8):
        self.params = list(params)
        self.lr, self.alpha, self.eps = lr, alpha, eps
        self.v = [torch.zeros_like(p) for p in self.params]  # EMA of g²

    def step(self):
        for p, v in zip(self.params, self.v):
            if p.grad is None: continue
            v.mul_(self.alpha).addcmul_(p.grad, p.grad, value=1-self.alpha)
            # v = α*v + (1-α)*g²
            p.data.sub_(self.lr * p.grad / (v.sqrt() + self.eps))

# Centered RMSProp: normalise by var(g) instead of E[g²]
# μ_t = α*μ_{t-1} + (1-α)*g_t
# v_t = α*v_{t-1} + (1-α)*g_t²
# update: g / sqrt(v_t - μ_t² + ε)
optimizer_centered = torch.optim.RMSprop(
    model.parameters(), lr=1e-3, alpha=0.99, centered=True
)

eli3: RMSProp (Root Mean Square Propagation) solves AdaGrad's dying learning rate problem. Instead of accumulating all past squared gradients, it uses an exponential moving average — recent gradients matter more, old ones fade away.

Think of it as a rolling average of how 'noisy' each parameter's gradient has been recently. If a parameter has had consistently large gradients, its effective learning rate stays small. If gradients suddenly become smaller (you're near a good value), the effective learning rate recovers, unlike AdaGrad where it only ever shrinks.
RMSProp was invented by Geoff Hinton in a Coursera lecture in 2012 — it was never formally published! It's the key building block of Adam: Adam = RMSProp + momentum + bias correction.

whenWhy.title: Recurrent neural networks and non-stationary objectives
whenWhy.body: RMSProp was originally proposed for training RNNs on non-stationary problems (Hinton, 2012 Coursera lecture). RNN gradients vary wildly across timesteps — AdaGrad's monotone decay causes learning to stop before the model converges. RMSProp's exponential forgetting adapts to the current gradient scale, keeping the learning rate useful throughout training.

whenWhy.title: Reinforcement learning (policy gradient methods)
whenWhy.body: In RL, the gradient signal is noisy and non-stationary (the reward function changes as the policy improves). OpenAI's original PPO and A3C implementations used RMSProp. The adaptive scaling helps stabilise training when reward variance is high. Many modern RL implementations have since switched to Adam or AdamW.

whenWhy.title: When Adam is unavailable (memory constraints)
whenWhy.body: Adam requires two moment buffers (m and v) per parameter = 3× model memory including weights. RMSProp requires only one buffer (v) = 2× model memory. For very large models or limited-memory settings, RMSProp is a lower-memory alternative with most of Adam's adaptive learning rate benefit (without the first moment / momentum component).

deepDiveIntro: RMSProp maintains an exponential moving average of squared gradients and divides each gradient update by the square root of that average. At stationarity, the moving average converges to the true mean squared gradient, so the effective learning rate stabilizes rather than shrinking to zero as in AdaGrad. The decay rate controls the memory length — a value close to one gives a long memory, a value close to zero adapts quickly to recent gradient scale changes. [1]

Centered RMSProp (Graves, 2013) additionally maintains an exponential moving average of raw gradients, then subtracts its square from the squared-gradient average before taking the square root. This estimates the variance rather than the second moment of the gradient, which is a more principled normalization when the mean gradient is non-zero. [2]
formula: vₜ = γvₜ₋₁ + (1−γ)gₜ²;  wₜ = wₜ₋₁ − η·gₜ/√(vₜ+ε)

deepDive.heading: Update Rule
deepDive.body: The moving average of squared gradients is bounded rather than monotonically increasing, so the effective per-parameter learning rate stabilizes at the step size divided by the root mean squared gradient. For stationary gradients the moving average converges to the true mean squared gradient, giving a stable effective learning rate throughout training. A decay rate of 0.99 gives roughly a hundred-step memory; 0.9 gives roughly a ten-step memory. [1]

deepDive.heading: RMSProp vs AdaGrad: Dying Learning Rate
deepDive.body: AdaGrad accumulates all squared gradients from the start of training, so its denominator grows without bound and the effective learning rate shrinks to zero. RMSProp replaces this cumulative sum with an exponential moving average, so old gradients lose influence as training continues. If the gradient magnitude decreases near the minimum, the moving average decreases too and the effective learning rate recovers — the opposite of what AdaGrad does. This non-monotone learning rate behavior is critical for long training runs. A decay rate of 0.99 gives a gradient half-life of about 69 steps. [1]

deepDive.heading: RMSProp + Momentum
deepDive.body: PyTorch RMSProp supports an optional momentum term that applies a velocity-style moving average to the normalized gradient update rather than to the raw gradient. This brings RMSProp close to Adam in behavior but without the bias correction that Adam applies to both its first and second moment estimates. The bias correction matters most in the first hundred steps or so, after which the two optimizers behave similarly. Empirically, RMSProp with momentum approximates Adam without bias correction on most supervised learning tasks. [1]

takeaways: vₜ = γvₜ₋₁ + (1−γ)gₜ²; wₜ = wₜ₋₁ − η·gₜ/√(vₜ+ε) — EMA fixes AdaGrad's dying lr. [1]
vₜ converges to E[gₜ²] at stationarity — effective lr stabilises at η/√E[g²]. [1]
Centered RMSProp normalises by gradient std (√(v−μ²)) — more principled for non-zero mean. [2]
Adam = RMSProp + momentum (first moment) + bias correction. [1]
Lower memory than Adam (1 buffer vs 2) — useful for large models with tight memory budgets. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is the effect of γ (alpha) in RMSProp and how should it be tuned?
edgeCases.a: γ controls the memory length of the second moment estimate. The half-life of past gradients is log(0.5)/log(γ) ≈ 69/(1−γ) steps. γ=0.9: 69-step memory — adapts quickly to changing gradient scales. γ=0.99: 690-step memory — smooth, stable estimate. γ=0.999: 6900-step memory — very slow adaptation. For non-stationary problems (RL, RNNs), use γ=0.9 for fast adaptation. For stable supervised learning, γ=0.99–0.999 gives better gradient scale estimates. Note: Adam uses β₂=0.999 for the same second moment.

edgeCases.q: Why is RMSProp not formally published, and does that affect trust in it?
edgeCases.a: Hinton introduced RMSProp in Lecture 6 of his Coursera 'Neural Networks for Machine Learning' (2012). There is no paper — just a 2-slide derivation. Despite this, RMSProp has been extensively validated empirically: Graves (2013) used it for handwriting generation RNNs; DeepMind used it in the original DQN; TensorFlow and PyTorch both implement it. The lack of a paper with theoretical guarantees is worth noting — RMSProp has no general convergence proof for non-convex problems, unlike Adam (which has asymptotic guarantees under mild assumptions).

sources.label: Hinton, G. (2012) — Neural Networks for Machine Learning, Lecture 6e (Coursera)
Graves, A. (2013) — Generating Sequences With Recurrent Neural Networks arXiv:1308.0850
